Spring 2026" format: revealjs: theme: default highlight-style: github incremental: false fig-width: 9 fig-height: 3.5 scrollable: true slide-number: true execute: echo: true warning: false --- ## NOTES {visibility="hidden"} - NEED TO GET DATA/SIM FROM COURSE REPO TO FOLLOW ALONG - remove `purrr::compose()` - explain `dplyr::nest_by()` better # Batch Load Data & List Columns **Week 4** ## Agenda * Review Lab 2 * `map_dfr` * List columns * Batch-loading data * Midterm Student Survey ## Learning objectives {.smaller} * Understand when `map_dfr` can and should be applied * Better understand file paths, and how `{fs}` can help * Be able to batch load data of a specific type within a mixed-type directory * Use file names to pull data * Introduce list columns * Contrast: + `group_by() |> nest() |> mutate() |> map()` with + `nest_by() |> summarize()` * Understand list columns and how they relate to `base::split` * Fluently nest/unnest data frames * Understand why `tidyr::nest()` can be a powerful framework (data frames) and when `tidyr::unnest()` can/should be used to move out of nested data frames and into a standard data frame # Review Lab 2 # `map_dfr()` ## `map_dfr()` If each iteration returns a data frame, you can use `map_dfr` to automatically bind all the data frames together ## Example Create a function that simulates data (please copy the code and follow along) ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(3000) simulate <- function(n, mean = 0, sd = 1) { tibble(sample_id = seq_len(n), sample = rnorm(n, mean, sd)) } simulate(10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 10 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 1.26 2 2 0.645 3 3 0.463 4 4 0.770 5 5 -0.226 6 6 -0.352 7 7 -0.649 8 8 -1.26 9 9 -1.70 10 10 -1.83 ``` ::: ::: ## Two more quick examples :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} simulate(3, 100, 10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 91.8 2 2 83.8 3 3 103. ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} simulate(5, -10, 1.5) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 -7.76 2 2 -9.96 3 3 -10.8 4 4 -9.32 5 5 -9.10 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Simulation * Assume we want to vary the sample size from 10 to 150 by increments of 5 * `mean` stays constant at 100 * `sd` is constant at 10 ## Try with `purrr::map()` ``{=html} ## ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyverse) sims <- map(seq(10, 150, 5), simulate, mean = 100, sd = 10) ``` ::: :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sims[1] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] # A tibble: 10 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 111. 2 2 80.4 3 3 99.5 4 4 110. 5 5 93.5 6 6 95.4 7 7 89.7 8 8 92.7 9 9 106. 10 10 94.4 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sims[2] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] # A tibble: 15 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 87.4 2 2 93.7 3 3 96.6 4 4 104. 5 5 110. 6 6 91.6 7 7 110. 8 8 99.7 9 9 96.5 10 10 99.2 11 11 77.5 12 12 108. 13 13 75.2 14 14 113. 15 15 105. ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Swap for `map_dfr` Try it - what happens? ``{=html} . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sims_df <- map_dfr(seq(10, 150, 5), simulate, 100, 10) sims_df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2,320 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 93.6 2 2 108. 3 3 107. 4 4 92.6 5 5 112. 6 6 90.6 7 7 107. 8 8 117. 9 9 106. 10 10 99.2 # ℹ 2,310 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Notice a problem here? {.smaller} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sims_df[1:26, ] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 26 × 2 sample_id sample 1 1 93.6 2 2 108. 3 3 107. 4 4 92.6 5 5 112. 6 6 90.6 7 7 107. 8 8 117. 9 9 106. 10 10 99.2 # ℹ 16 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## `.id` Either a string or `NULL` + If a string, the output will contain a variable with that name, storing either the name (if `.x` is named) or the index (if `.x` is unnamed) of the input. + If `NULL`, the default, no variable will be created. ::: aside [{purrr} documentation](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/reference/map.html) ::: ## `.id` string unnamed ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sims_df2 <- map_dfr(seq(10, 150, 5), simulate, 100, 10, .id = "iteration") sims_df2[1:15, ] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 3 iteration sample_id sample 1 1 1 100. 2 1 2 108. 3 1 3 98.9 4 1 4 106. 5 1 5 103. 6 1 6 119. 7 1 7 109. 8 1 8 116. 9 1 9 102. 10 1 10 82.9 11 2 1 86.0 12 2 2 98.1 13 2 3 126. 14 2 4 95.4 15 2 5 102. ``` ::: ::: ## `.id` named string {.smaller} `setNames()` - sets the names on an object and returns the object `setNames(object = nm, nm)` - `nm` = a character vector of names to assign to the object . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} sample_size <- setNames(seq(10, 150, 5), english::english(seq(10, 150, 5))) sample_size[1:15] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ten fifteen twenty twenty-five thirty thirty-five 10 15 20 25 30 35 forty forty-five fifty fifty-five sixty sixty-five 40 45 50 55 60 65 seventy seventy-five eighty 70 75 80 ``` ::: ::: ## Try again ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sims_df3 <- map_dfr(sample_size, simulate, 100, 10, .id = "n") sims_df3[1:15, ] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 3 n sample_id sample 1 ten 1 99.7 2 ten 2 109. 3 ten 3 119. 4 ten 4 97.9 5 ten 5 99.9 6 ten 6 101. 7 ten 7 80.5 8 ten 8 98.3 9 ten 9 101. 10 ten 10 96.7 11 fifteen 1 98.0 12 fifteen 2 93.2 13 fifteen 3 102. 14 fifteen 4 101. 15 fifteen 5 103. ``` ::: ::: ## `broom::tidy()` The `{broom}` package helps us extract model output in a tidy format ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lm(tvhours ~ age, gss_cat) |> broom::tidy() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 5 term estimate std.error statistic p.value 1 (Intercept) 1.99 0.0698 28.5 4.67e-173 2 age 0.0210 0.00139 15.1 4.69e- 51 ``` ::: ::: ## Fit separate models by year Again - probably not the best *statistically* ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} split(gss_cat, gss_cat$year) |> map_dfr( ~lm(tvhours ~ age, .x) |> broom::tidy() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 16 × 5 term estimate std.error statistic p.value 1 (Intercept) 2.08 0.171 12.2 8.00e-33 2 age 0.0195 0.00349 5.59 2.60e- 8 3 (Intercept) ![](https://media0.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExaTVhbW1mbm03NWQ5Zmx4M2JlNWVqMHIwYmtjdnRkOGgyb3JqOTYxcyZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/xT9IgEldeKnHa9oOCQ/giphy.gif) ## Challenge {.smaller} * Output the mean score for students who were coded as not having a disability (code 0), along with students coded as having TBI. * In other words, continue the previous slide, but output a data frame with three columns: (1) `content` (2) `intercept`, and (3) `tbi` coefficient (which is primary code 74, "*primary74*") ``{=html} ## Solution . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} by_content |> mutate( m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)), coefs = map(m1, coef), no_disab = map_dbl(coefs, 1), tbi = map_dbl(coefs, "primary74") ) |> select(content, no_disab, tbi) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 # Groups: content [5] content no_disab tbi 1 ELA 0.932 -0.765 2 Math -0.159 0.350 3 Rdg 1.36 0.266 4 Science 1.49 1.30 5 Wri 1.57 -0.404 ``` ::: ::: ## Solution To be pedantic (accurate) about it, to get the effect of the `tbi` variable, we'd need to add the `intercept` to the `tbi` coefficient (don't worry about this if you don't do regression) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} by_content |> mutate( m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)), coefs = map(m1, coef), no_disab = map_dbl(coefs, 1), tbi = no_disab + map_dbl(coefs, "primary74") ) |> select(content, no_disab, tbi) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 # Groups: content [5] content no_disab tbi 1 ELA 0.932 0.167 2 Math -0.159 0.191 3 Rdg 1.36 1.63 4 Science 1.49 2.79 5 Wri 1.57 1.17 ``` ::: ::: ## Compare models Back to our original task - fit all three models You try first 1. `lm(Theta ~ primary)` 2. `lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary)` 3. `lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary + primary:secondary)` ``{=html} ## Model fits . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} mods <- by_content |> mutate( m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)), m2 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary, data = .x)), m3 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary * secondary, data = .x)) ) mods ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 5 # Groups: content [5] content data m1 m2 m3 1 ELA 2 Math 3 Rdg 4 Science 5 Wri ``` ::: ::: ## Brief walk into parallel iterations `stats::anova()` function can compare the fit of two models - computes analysis of variance (ANOVA) tables for one or more fitted model objects - test whether the model terms are significant - provide a summary for a single model's significance - or compare multiple models against each other ## But first... How would we extract model 1 and model 2 for ELA data? . . . :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods$m1[[1]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = Theta ~ primary, data = .x) Coefficients: (Intercept) primary10 primary20 primary40 primary50 primary60 0.93223 0.38570 -0.03168 -1.84434 1.17372 0.77625 primary70 primary74 primary80 primary82 primary90 -1.83026 -0.76479 0.46765 0.33825 1.47936 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods$m2[[1]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = Theta ~ primary + secondary, data = .x) Coefficients: (Intercept) primary10 primary20 primary40 primary50 primary60 1.0043 0.4285 0.2807 -1.6282 1.1147 0.8676 primary70 primary74 primary80 primary82 primary90 secondary10 -1.4430 -0.1168 0.5283 0.3298 1.4101 -0.5685 secondary20 secondary40 secondary43 secondary50 secondary60 secondary70 -1.0558 -2.2707 -1.7943 0.2484 0.4831 -1.3336 secondary74 secondary80 secondary82 secondary90 -6.3695 -0.5142 -0.7259 1.6000 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## So which fits better? {.smaller} ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} compare <- anova(mods$m1[[1]], mods$m2[[1]]) compare ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Analysis of Variance Table Model 1: Theta ~ primary Model 2: Theta ~ primary + secondary Res.Df RSS Df Sum of Sq F Pr(>F) 1 3616 20905 2 3605 20100 11 804.26 13.113 < 2.2e-16 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ::: . . . `Pr(>F)` $p$ < 0.001 so we reject the null hypothesis (means are equal) and conclude that group means differ ## `map2()` {.smaller} ::: {.incremental} * Works the same as `map` but iterates over two vectors concurrently + You heard that right, iterates over two vectors concurrently! * Let's compare model 1 and 2 ::: . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods |> mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 6 # Groups: content [5] content data m1 m2 m3 comp12 1 ELA 2 Math 3 Rdg 4 Science 5 Wri ``` ::: ::: . . . Ok...now what? ## Back to our original `anova()` object Can we pull out useful things? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} str(compare) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Classes 'anova' and 'data.frame': 2 obs. of 6 variables: $ Res.Df : num 3616 3605 $ RSS : num 20905 20100 $ Df : num NA 11 $ Sum of Sq: num NA 804 $ F : num NA 13.1 $ Pr(>F) : num NA 7.66e-25 - attr(*, "heading")= chr [1:2] "Analysis of Variance Table\n" "Model 1: Theta ~ primary\nModel 2: Theta ~ primary + secondary" ``` ::: ::: . . . Try pulling out the $p$-value ## Extract $p$ value *Note* - I'd recommend looking at more than just a $p$-value, but I do think this is useful for a quick glance ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} compare$`Pr(>F)` ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] NA 7.663566e-25 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} compare[["Pr(>F)"]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] NA 7.663566e-25 ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} compare$`Pr(>F)`[2] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 7.663566e-25 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} compare[["Pr(>F)"]][2] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 7.663566e-25 ``` ::: ::: ## Back to our list columns {.smaller} Extract all $p$-values This is probably the most compact syntax, but that doesn't mean it's the most clear ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods |> mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova), p12 = map_dbl(comp12, list("Pr(>F)", 2))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 7 # Groups: content [5] content data m1 m2 m3 comp12 p12 1 ELA 7.66e-25 2 Math 1.72e-22 3 Rdg 1.53e-28 4 Science 4.69e-18 5 Wri 5.79e-11 ``` ::: ::: . . . I'd probably do ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods |> mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova), p12 = map_dbl(comp12, ~.x[["Pr(>F)"]][2])) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 7 # Groups: content [5] content data m1 m2 m3 comp12 p12 1 ELA 7.66e-25 2 Math 1.72e-22 3 Rdg 1.53e-28 4 Science 4.69e-18 5 Wri 5.79e-11 ``` ::: ::: ## Another approach Write a function that pulls the $p$-value from model comparison objects . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fx_extractp <- function(anova_ob) { anova_ob[["Pr(>F)"]][2] } ``` ::: . . . Loop this function through the anova objects ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|3"} mods |> mutate(comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova), p12 = map_dbl(comp12, fx_extractp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 7 # Groups: content [5] content data m1 m2 m3 comp12 p12 1 ELA 7.66e-25 2 Math 1.72e-22 3 Rdg 1.53e-28 4 Science 4.69e-18 5 Wri 5.79e-11 ``` ::: ::: ## Brief sidetrack We can also create the function using `purrr::compose()` . . . ![](https://media4.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExb3NycWJ2Mmd0aDBtdmYwZnh3Z2JnM3EyZWh1OGg1OXJmamVkbTdoNiZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/f6cyISseQU9IoW9wW5/giphy.gif) ## `compose()` {.smaller} [`compose()`](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/reference/compose.html) Create a new function that is the composition of multiple functions - i.e., `compose(f, g)` is equivalent to `f(g(...))` . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} not_null <- compose(`!`, is.null) ``` ::: . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} not_null(4) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] TRUE ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} not_null(NULL) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] FALSE ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} add1 <- function(x) x + 1 compose(add1, add1)(8) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 10 ``` ::: ::: ## `compose()` example Create a centering function (which subtracts the mean from each observation) Use `~` and `.x`, just like with the `map()` functions ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fx_center <- compose(~.x - mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: ## Test it out ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} library(palmerpenguins) penguins$bill_length_mm |> head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 39.1 39.5 40.3 NA 36.7 39.3 ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|2"} penguins$bill_length_mm |> fx_center() |> head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] -4.82193 -4.42193 -3.62193 NA -7.22193 -4.62193 ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins$bill_length_mm |> fx_center() |> mean(na.rm = TRUE) |> round() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0 ``` ::: ::: ## Compose a $p$-value extractor ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fx_p <- compose(~.x[["Pr(>F)"]][2]) ``` ::: . . . Apply it ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} mods |> mutate( comp12 = map2(m1, m2, anova), p12 = map_dbl(comp12, fx_p) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 7 # Groups: content [5] content data m1 m2 m3 comp12 p12 1 ELA 7.66e-25 2 Math 1.72e-22 3 Rdg 1.53e-28 4 Science 4.69e-18 5 Wri 5.79e-11 ``` ::: ::: ## Functions * This was a quick intro * Don't worry if it doesn't really make sense yet * We'll talk about them a lot more in the coming weeks # Conducting operations by row A `{purrr}` alternative ## Operations by row {.smaller} The `dplyr::rowwise()` function fundamentally changes the way a `tibble()` behaves ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} (df <- tibble(name = c("Me", "You"), x = 1:2, y = 3:4, z = 5:6)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 4 name x y z 1 Me 1 3 5 2 You 2 4 6 ``` ::: ::: . . . :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> mutate(m = mean(c(x, y, z))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 5 name x y z m 1 Me 1 3 5 3.5 2 You 2 4 6 3.5 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|2"} df |> rowwise() |> mutate(m = mean(c(x, y, z))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 5 # Rowwise: name x y z m 1 Me 1 3 5 3 2 You 2 4 6 4 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Add a group & summarize :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> rowwise() |> summarize(m = mean(c(x, y, z))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 1 m 1 3 2 4 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> rowwise(name) |> summarize(m = mean(c(x, y, z))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 2 # Groups: name [2] name m 1 Me 3 2 You 4 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## List columns {.smaller} If you apply `rowwise()` operation with a list column, you don't have to loop ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} (df <- tibble(var = list(1, 2:3, 4:6))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 1 var 1 2 3 ``` ::: ::: . . . :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} `map_` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> mutate( lngth = map_int(var, length) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 2 var lngth 1 1 2 2 3 3 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%".fragment} `rowwise()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} df |> rowwise() |> mutate(lnght = length(var)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 2 # Rowwise: var lnght 1 1 2 2 3 3 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: # List columns again ## Creating list columns {.smaller} You can use the `dplyr::nest_by()` function to create a list column for each group, *and* convert it to a rowwise data frame . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} d |> nest_by(content) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 2 # Rowwise: content content data > 1 ELA [3,627 × 26] 2 Math [3,629 × 26] 3 Rdg [3,627 × 26] 4 Science [1,435 × 26] 5 Wri [3,627 × 26] ``` ::: ::: . . . Change the column name ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} d |> nest_by(content, .key = "items") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 2 # Rowwise: content content items > 1 ELA [3,627 × 26] 2 Math [3,629 × 26] 3 Rdg [3,627 × 26] 4 Science [1,435 × 26] 5 Wri [3,627 × 26] ``` ::: ::: ## Challenge {.smaller} Can you fit a model of the form `lm(Theta ~ primary)` to each content area in the `d` object we created using `nest_by()`, **NOT** using `{purrr}`? ``{=html} . . . Wrap it in `list()` (should suggest this in the error reporting if you didn't do it) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} d |> nest_by(content) |> mutate(m1 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary, data = data))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 3 # Rowwise: content content data m1 > 1 ELA [3,627 × 26] 2 Math [3,629 × 26] 3 Rdg [3,627 × 26] 4 Science [1,435 × 26] 5 Wri [3,627 × 26] ``` ::: ::: ## Another Challenge {.smaller} Can you extend it further and extract the coefficients with `coef`? What about creating a new column that has the intercept values? ``{=html} . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} d |> nest_by(content) |> mutate(m1 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary, data = data)), coefs = list(coef(m1)), intercept = coef(m1)[1]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 5 # Rowwise: content content data m1 coefs intercept > 1 ELA [3,627 × 26] 0.932 2 Math [3,629 × 26] -0.159 3 Rdg [3,627 × 26] 1.36 4 Science [1,435 × 26] 1.49 5 Wri [3,627 × 26] 1.57 ``` ::: ::: ## Fit all models This gets us the same results we got before: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods2 <- d |> nest_by(content) |> mutate( m1 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary, data = data)), m2 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary, data = data)), m3 = list(lm(Theta ~ primary * secondary, data = data)) ) mods2 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 5 # Rowwise: content content data m1 m2 m3 > 1 ELA [3,627 × 26] 2 Math [3,629 × 26] 3 Rdg [3,627 × 26] 4 Science [1,435 × 26] 5 Wri [3,627 × 26] ``` ::: ::: ## Look at all $R^2$ It's a normal tidy data frame! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mods |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 4 # Groups: content [5] content data model output 1 ELA m1 2 ELA m2 3 ELA m3 4 Math m1 5 Math m2 6 Math m3 7 Rdg m1 8 Rdg m2 9 Rdg m3 10 Science m1 11 Science m2 12 Science m3 13 Wri m1 14 Wri m2 15 Wri m3 ``` ::: ::: ## Extract all $R^2$ {.smaller} *Note*: might rather want to write a function here ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} r2 <- mods |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) |> mutate(r2 = map_dbl(output, ~summary(.x)$r.squared)) r2 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 5 # Groups: content [5] content data model output r2 1 ELA m1 0.0452 2 ELA m2 0.0819 3 ELA m3 0.116 4 Math m1 0.0533 5 Math m2 0.0868 6 Math m3 0.119 7 Rdg m1 0.0481 8 Rdg m2 0.0893 9 Rdg m3 0.122 10 Science m1 0.0868 11 Science m2 0.152 12 Science m3 0.217 13 Wri m1 0.0517 14 Wri m2 0.0698 15 Wri m3 0.0982 ``` ::: ::: ## Plot {.smaller} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(r2, aes(model, r2)) + geom_col(aes(fill = model)) + facet_wrap(~content) + guides(fill = "none") + scale_fill_manual(values = met.brewer("Egypt", 3)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w4_files/figure-revealjs/model-plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ## Unnesting * Sometimes you just want to `unnest()` * Imagine we want to plot the coefficients by model... how? * `broom::tidy()` & `tidyr::unnest()` ## Tidy ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|7"} mods |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) |> mutate(tidied = map(output, broom::tidy)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 5 # Groups: content [5] content data model output tidied 1 ELA m1 2 ELA m2 3 ELA m3 4 Math m1 5 Math m2 6 Math m3 7 Rdg m1 8 Rdg m2 9 Rdg m3 10 Science m1 11 Science m2 12 Science m3 13 Wri m1 14 Wri m2 15 Wri m3 ``` ::: ::: ## Equivalently ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|7|8"} mods |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) |> rowwise() |> mutate(tidied = list(broom::tidy(output))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 5 # Rowwise: content content data model output tidied 1 ELA m1 2 ELA m2 3 ELA m3 4 Math m1 5 Math m2 6 Math m3 7 Rdg m1 8 Rdg m2 9 Rdg m3 10 Science m1 11 Science m2 12 Science m3 13 Wri m1 14 Wri m2 15 Wri m3 ``` ::: ::: ## Select and unnest ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|8|9"} tidied <- mods |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) |> mutate(tidy_broom = map(output, broom::tidy)) |> select(content, model, tidy_broom) |> unnest(tidy_broom) tidied ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 841 × 7 # Groups: content [5] content model term estimate std.error statistic p.value 1 ELA m1 (Intercept) 0.932 0.215 4.33 0.0000150 2 ELA m1 primary10 0.386 0.224 1.72 0.0856 3 ELA m1 primary20 -0.0317 0.727 -0.0436 0.965 4 ELA m1 primary40 -1.84 0.556 -3.32 0.000916 5 ELA m1 primary50 1.17 0.289 4.06 0.0000500 6 ELA m1 primary60 0.776 0.387 2.01 0.0447 7 ELA m1 primary70 -1.83 0.309 -5.93 0.00000000330 8 ELA m1 primary74 -0.765 0.518 -1.48 0.140 9 ELA m1 primary80 0.468 0.243 1.93 0.0542 10 ELA m1 primary82 0.338 0.227 1.49 0.136 # ℹ 831 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Plot Lets look how the primary coefficients change ::: {.cell output-location='slide'} ```{.r .cell-code} to_plot <- names(coef(mods$m1[[1]])) tidied |> filter(term %in% to_plot) |> ggplot(aes(estimate, term, color = model)) + geom_point() + scale_color_manual(values = met.brewer("Egypt", 3)) + facet_wrap(~content, nrow = 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w4_files/figure-revealjs/coef-plot-echo-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ## Last bit * We've kind of been running the wrong models this whole time * We forgot about "*grade*"! * No problem, just change the grouping factor ## By grade ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} by_grade_content <- d |> group_by(content, grade) |> nest() by_grade_content ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 31 × 3 # Groups: content, grade [31] grade content data 1 11 ELA 2 11 Math 3 11 Rdg 4 11 Science 5 11 Wri 6 3 ELA 7 3 Math 8 3 Rdg 9 3 Wri 10 4 ELA # ℹ 21 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Fit models ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} mods_grade <- by_grade_content |> mutate( m1 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary, data = .x)), m2 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary + secondary, data = .x)), m3 = map(data, ~lm(Theta ~ primary * secondary, data = .x)) ) mods_grade ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 31 × 6 # Groups: content, grade [31] grade content data m1 m2 m3 1 11 ELA 2 11 Math 3 11 Rdg 4 11 Science 5 11 Wri 6 3 ELA 7 3 Math 8 3 Rdg 9 3 Wri 10 4 ELA # ℹ 21 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Look at $R^2$ ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} mods_grade |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) |> mutate(r2 = map_dbl(output, ~summary(.x)$r.squared)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 93 × 6 # Groups: content, grade [31] grade content data model output r2 1 11 ELA m1 0.0335 2 11 ELA m2 0.108 3 11 ELA m3 0.154 4 11 Math m1 0.189 5 11 Math m2 0.316 6 11 Math m3 0.405 7 11 Rdg m1 0.0207 8 11 Rdg m2 0.182 9 11 Rdg m3 0.234 10 11 Science m1 0.126 # ℹ 83 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Plot {.smaller} ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} mods_grade |> pivot_longer( m1:m3, names_to = "model", values_to = "output" ) |> mutate(r2 = map_dbl(output, ~summary(.x)$r.squared)) |> ggplot(aes(model, r2)) + geom_col(aes(fill = model)) + facet_grid(grade ~ content) + guides(fill = "none") + scale_fill_manual(values = met.brewer("Egypt", 3)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w4_files/figure-revealjs/by_grade_r2_plot-echo-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ## Summary {.smaller} * List columns are really powerful and really flexible * Also help you stay organized * You can approach the problem either with `{purrr}` or `dplyr::rowwise()` + **Important**: If you use `rowwise()`, remember to `ungroup()` when you want it to go back to being a normal data frame + `rowwise()` might be a bit easier but is a little less general (only works with data frames) + I always use `{purrr}` # Next time ## Before next class - No readings! - Midterm Student Survey now # Midterm Student Survey ## Midterm Student Survey - Log onto Canvas - Click "UO Course Surveys" on the left bar - Complete survey

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
